alloy_matches.reason: Same alloy name, base element, alloying elements, and nominal composition (Fe balance vs 73.4% are equivalent).

process_matches.reason: Both describe vacuum induction melting, homogenization at 1373 K/2h, hot rolling five passes finishing 1173 K, air cooling to 3 mm sheet, linked to same alloy.

sample_matches.reason: Referenced alloy and process match, context describes same triplex microstructure (austenite, δ-ferrite, TiB2).

truth.papers.A62.title: Low-density and high-modulus steel achieved by hypereutectic TiB2 and high Al ferrite phase
truth.papers.A62.paper_scope: Design and characterization of a novel low-density steel with hypereutectic TiB2 and high-Al δ-ferrite phase to achieve low density, high Young's modulus, and balanced mechanical properties.

prediction.papers.A62.title: Low-density and high-modulus steel achieved by hypereutectic TiB2 and high Al ferrite phase
prediction.papers.A62.paper_scope: Design of a carbon-free austenite-based low-density triplex steel containing in-situ TiB2 and δ-ferrite bands in an austenite matrix, achieving low density (6.88 g cm^-3), high Young's modulus (225 GPa), and superior strength-ductility balance via conventional steel processing, with systematic investigation of deformation mechanisms through experiments and FEM simulations.

prediction.alloys.alloy_fe15_5mn3_95al5ti2_15b.allo: Actual measured composition of the rolled sheet is Fe-16Mn-3.9Al-5.11Ti-2.09B (wt.%). Designed to contain ~14 vol.% TiB2 in a matrix of δ-ferrite and austenite.

truth.processes.proc_fe15_5mn3_95al5ti2_15b_hr: Vacuum induction melting of 50 kg ingot, homogenization at 1373 K for 2 h, hot rolling in five passes with finishing temperature 1173 K, air cooling to room temperature, producing 3 mm thick sheet.
Hot rolling performed in two-phase region; final sheet thickness 3 mm.

prediction.processes.proc_fe15_5mn3_95al5ti2_15b_hr: Vacuum induction melting of a 50 kg ingot, homogenization at 1373 K for 2 h, hot rolling in the two-phase region in five steps with a finishing temperature of 1173 K to a 3 mm thick sheet, followed by air cooling to room temperature.
Phase diagram calculations (Thermo-Calc, TCFE9) were used to design the composition for ~14 vol.% TiB2 in a dual-phase matrix.

truth.processing_steps.sample_lds_hr.processin: Finishing temperature 1173 K, rolled in two-phase region

prediction.processing_steps.sample_lds_hr.processin: Five rolling steps in the two-phase region to produce a 3 mm thick sheet, finishing temperature 1173 K, followed by air cooling to room temperature.

truth.structures.sample_lds_hr.overall_structu: Heterogeneous triplex microstructure: equiaxed austenite matrix embedded with elongated δ-ferrite bands and micron-sized TiB2 particles.
truth.structures.sample_lds_hr.microstructure_: irregular particles, primary (hexagonal prisms) and eutectic (plate-like/needle-like)
high in δ-ferrite near TiB2 interfaces, lower at austenite/TiB2 interfaces
austenite grain size ~3.9 μm (equiaxed), δ-ferrite grain size ~4.4 μm (elongated), δ-ferrite band thickness ~4.3 μm, austenite layer thickness ~12.1 μm
mainly at grain/phase boundaries, avg ~15 μm, coarse >10 μm dominate volume fraction (>80%)

prediction.structures.sample_lds_hr.overall_structu: Heterogeneous triplex microstructure comprising a fully equiaxed-austenitic matrix embedded with elongated δ-ferrite bands and micron-sized TiB2 particles at grain/phase boundaries.
prediction.structures.sample_lds_hr.microstructure_: elongated bands parallel to rolling direction, average band thickness ~4.3 μm
hexagonal prisms (primary) and irregular plate-like/needle-like debris (eutectic)
Initial state: δ-ferrite (1.1±0.2)×10^15 m^-2, austenite (3±0.7)×10^14 m^-2. After tensile fracture: δ-ferrite (1.7±0.7)×10^15 m^-2, austenite (2±0.3)×10^15 m^-2.
Bimodal matrix: austenite equiaxed ~3.9 μm; δ-ferrite elongated ~4.4 μm with band thickness ~4.3 μm.

truth.interfaces.sample_lds_hr.defect_interact: High dislocation density at δ-ferrite/TiB2 interfaces, forming dense dislocation tangles.
Lower dislocation density at austenite/TiB2 interfaces compared to δ-ferrite/TiB2.
truth.interfaces.sample_lds_hr.interface_notes: Strain concentration observed at TiB2/matrix interfaces, especially at δ-ferrite/TiB2 and austenite/TiB2 boundaries, leading to strain gradients and GND formation.

prediction.interfaces.sample_lds_hr.defect_interact: Dense dislocation tangles at δ-ferrite/TiB2 interfaces; sparse dislocation tangles at austenite/TiB2 interfaces. All TiB2 particles are exclusively located at grain or phase boundaries.
prediction.interfaces.sample_lds_hr.interface_notes: Local von Mises strain concentrations of ~10% at small TiB2/δ-ferrite interfaces, ~16% at austenite/TiB2 boundaries, and up to ~20% near sharp TiB2/austenite and TiB2/δ-ferrite boundaries at higher deformation; strain localizations oriented at ~45° to the loading axis.
prediction.interfaces.sample_lds_hr.phase_evolution: TiB2 particles formed during liquid-phase solidification (primary and eutectic); both types underwent fragmentation during hot rolling but retained inherited morphologies. δ-ferrite formed during solidification and persisted through hot rolling and air cooling. Austenite dynamically recrystallized during hot rolling, resulting in equiaxed grains.

truth.properties.sample_lds_hr.mechanical.tens: Engineering stress-strain curve presented in Fig. 3(a); true stress-strain curves used in FEM simulations.

prediction.properties.sample_lds_hr.mechanical.tens: Engineering stress-strain curve values for present steel at discrete strains: 5% ~600 MPa, 10% ~650 MPa, 15% ~670 MPa, 20% ~660 MPa, 25% ~650 MPa, 30% ~640 MPa. See Fig. 3(a) for comparison with conventional Fe-TiB2 and IF steels.

μ-DIC and FEM reveal strain localizations at ~45° to loading axis; high strain (~10%) at small TiB2/δ-ferrite interfaces, elevated strain (~16%) at austenite/TiB2 boundaries, and concentrations up to ~20% near sharp TiB2/matrix boundaries at higher plastic deformation.

truth.performance.sample_lds_hr.service_condit: uniaxial tension, nominal strain rate 1e-3 s^-1
truth.performance.sample_lds_hr.comparative_pe: 22% increase in yield strength (503 vs 389 MPa), 30% increase in UTS (672 vs 549 MPa), uniform elongation 13% vs 11%

prediction.performance.sample_lds_hr.comparative_pe: Yield strength 22% higher (503 vs 389 MPa), UTS 30% higher (672 vs 549 MPa), uniform elongation 13% vs 11%

truth.characterization_methods.sample_lds_hr.p: Simulate stress-strain partitioning, dislocation density evolution, and damage evolution
truth.characterization_methods.sample_lds_hr.k: Validated against experimental tensile curves and dislocation densities. Phase stress ranking: TiB2 > δ-ferrite > austenite. Heterogeneous microstructure promotes uniform plastic flow and delays shear band localization.

prediction.characterization_methods.sample_lds_hr.t: Quasi-static uniaxial tensile testing (SANS CMT-3000, nominal strain rate 1×10^-3 s^-1, room temperature)
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_lds_hr.k: Yield strength 503 MPa, UTS 672 MPa, uniform elongation 13% for dog-bone specimens with gauge length 25 mm and width 6 mm cut parallel to rolling direction.

truth.computational_details.sample_lds_hr.simu: Two-dimensional representative volume element (RVE) based on EBSD data, periodic boundary conditions, mesh size 0.4 μm (500×500 elements)
truth.computational_details.sample_lds_hr.vali: Simulated true stress-strain curves and dislocation density evolution closely match experimental results for both present steel and reference Fe-TiB2 steel.
truth.computational_details.sample_lds_hr.anal: Strain hardening in metallic phases (δ-ferrite and austenite)
k1: 230,000 (δ-ferrite), 200,000 (austenite); k2: 6 (δ-ferrite), 4.5 (austenite); combined with Taylor equation α=0.2, M=3.16/3.06, b=0.248/0.258 nm, μ=80.7/65 GPa
Dislocation density evolution couples with flow stress via Taylor equation; Hall-Petch strengthening also included.
Weibull fracture model for TiB2 particles
σc (critical stress) = 320 MPa, n (shape factor) = 1.5, k (damage scale) = 0.09, f0 = 0.1480, fd0 = 0.1245
Fracture probability depends on particle volume and internal defects.
Interface debonding between matrix and TiB2
Interface stiffness K = 10^6 MPa mm⁻¹, strength Tc = 700 MPa (δ-ferrite/TiB2), 640 MPa (austenite/TiB2)
Zero-thickness COH3D8 cohesive elements with bilinear traction-separation law.

prediction.computational_details.sample_lds_hr.simu: Finite element method with 2D EBSD-based RVE (200 μm × 200 μm, 500×500 CPE4 elements, 0.4 μm mesh size) and periodic boundary conditions; TiB2 treated as linearly elastic; matrix plasticity via Von Mises yield criterion with Taylor, Hall-Petch, and Kocks-Mecking hardening; damage via Weibull-distributed particle fracture and cohesive-zone interface debonding.
prediction.computational_details.sample_lds_hr.vali: Simulated true stress-strain curves and dislocation density evolutions closely match experimental measurements; strain distributions validated against μ-DIC results.
prediction.computational_details.sample_lds_hr.anal: Dislocation strengthening contribution: σ_dis = α M μ b √ρ
α=0.2; M=3.06 (austenite) / 3.16 (ferrite); μ=65 GPa (austenite) / 80.7 GPa (ferrite); b=0.258 nm (austenite) / 0.248 nm (ferrite)
kHP=460 MPa μm^-1/2 (austenite), 130 MPa μm^-1/2 (ferrite); d=3.9 μm (austenite), 4.4 μm (ferrite)
Evolution of dislocation density during straining: dρ/dε = M(k1√ρ - k2ρ)

truth.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Specific modulus (E/ρ) of the present steel is 32.7 GPa g⁻¹ cm³, which is higher than typical light metals (Al, Mg, Ti ~25 GPa g⁻¹ cm³).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Specific yield strength (SYS) = 73.1 MPa g⁻¹ cm³, specific ultimate tensile strength (SUTS) = 97.7 MPa g⁻¹ cm³.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: δ-ferrite contributes about 170 MPa to the overall flow stress (>30% of yield strength) as a stable strengthening baseline throughout deformation.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: The heterogeneous microstructure promotes additional geometrically necessary dislocations (GNDs) due to strain incompatibility between soft austenite and hard δ-ferrite, enhancing strain hardening.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: Compared to conventional Fe-TiB2 steel, the present steel exhibits a more uniform dislocation distribution and suppressed shear band formation, leading to improved ductility.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Particle fracture is a major damage mechanism; austenite-surrounded TiB2 particles show higher fracture frequency due to multi-directional shear strains from multiple grains.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: Crack tip blunting retards crack propagation from fractured TiB2 particles.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: Fractography reveals mixed fracture mode: ductile dimples, quasi-cleavage of TiB2, and particle/matrix decohesion.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: The alloy design intentionally avoids carbon to prevent TiC formation and uses B to form TiB2 with superior physical properties.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_9.value: The steel can be produced via conventional steel metallurgy (induction melting, hot rolling) without requiring advanced processing like splat cooling or additive manufacturing.

prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Specific modulus (E/ρ) is 32.7 GPa g^-1 cm^3, remarkably higher than typical ~25 GPa g^-1 cm^3 for light metals such as aluminum, magnesium, and titanium.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Specific yield strength (SYS) is 73.1 MPa g^-1 cm^3 and specific ultimate tensile strength (SUTS) is 97.7 MPa g^-1 cm^3.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Compared to typical Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (E 160–190 GPa with 8–10 wt.% Al), the present steel achieves 18%–40% higher Young's modulus at a comparable density (~6.88 g cm^-3) with only ~4 wt.% Al.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Compared to interstitial-free (IF) steel and the conventional Fe-TiB2 reference steel (σy=389 MPa, σu=549 MPa, UE=11%): the present steel shows nearly 100% higher yield strength than monolithic ferritic Fe-TiB2 steel reported in literature (257 MPa), and 22%/30% higher yield strength/UTS than the existing Fe-TiB2 reference with improved uniform elongation (13% vs 11%).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: Equilibrium phase fractions from Thermo-Calc (Fig. 1(a)): at 1050°C ferrite 0.4 + austenite 0.6 + TiB2 0.15; at 1200°C ferrite 0.85 + TiB2 0.15; at 1350°C liquid 0.4 + ferrite 0.6 + TiB2 0.15.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Damage initiation: first TiB2 particle fracture occurred at true plastic strain εp=0.0069 and applied true stress σa=556 MPa (exceeding yield stress 503 MPa). Austenite-surrounded TiB2 particles exhibited significantly higher fracture frequencies than ferrite-surrounded ones due to multi-directional shear strains from surrounding grains.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: Post-fracture analysis revealed numerous voids from cracking of large primary TiB2 particles (oriented nearly perpendicular to tensile axis) and substantial particle/matrix decohesion; fracture surface shows mixed mode of ductile dimples, quasi-cleavage of TiB2, and interfacial decohesion.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: FEM analysis indicates δ-ferrite provides a stable ~170 MPa strengthening contribution (>30% of overall yield strength) throughout deformation via composite effect, while austenite contributes increasing strain hardening due to rapid dislocation accumulation and geometrically necessary dislocations from phase heterogeneity.
